analysis_result: Only 2 of 4 MPNST cell lines (S462 and S462TY) showed significantly elevated GLI1 expression relative to HSC1λ. S462: 12.79-fold, p=0.0219; S462TY: 5.21-fold, p=0.0461; T265: 1.09-fold (NOT significant, p=0.8979); STS-26T: 0.24-fold (significantly REDUCED, p=0.0017). This partially confirms the paper's claim: S462 and S462TY are indeed significantly elevated, but T265 (despite a mean slightly above 1) does NOT reach statistical significance at p<0.05 in this 3-sample qPCR dataset due to high variance (values 0.78, 0.16, 2.34). STS-26T is significantly reduced, consistent with the paper treating it as the low-SHH control.
denominator_used: n=3 qPCR biological replicates per cell line (Sample 1, Sample 2, Sample 3); 4 MPNST lines tested (S462, S462TY, T265, STS-26T) vs reference HSC1λ (shown as 'HSC' in data, all values=1 by normalization convention).
assumptions_made: Values in Fig 5j sheet represent GLI1 qPCR fold-change relative to HSC1λ (per user-provided context; sheet only has one gene block with no explicit gene label); HSC column all-1 values reflect normalization to reference rather than measured variability (so Welch's t-test used, which accounts for zero variance in the reference group); significance threshold p<0.05 (two-sided, unadjusted for multiple comparisons across 4 lines); 'elevated' interpreted as mean fold-change > 1 with p<0.05.
step_by_step_analysis:
A. DATA LOADED
   - File: 41467_2023_38432_MOESM10_ESM.xlsx, sheet 'Fig 5j'
   - Dimensions: 4 rows x 6 columns (header + 3 samples; reference + 4 MPNST lines)
   - Columns: HSC (reference=HSC1λ), S462TY, STS-26T, T265, S462

B. COHORT DEFINITION
   - All three biological replicates (Sample 1, 2, 3) retained for each cell line
   - Final n=3 per line, 4 MPNST lines compared to HSC reference

C. VARIABLE CODING
   - Outcome: qPCR fold expression relative to HSC1λ
   - HSC values: [1, 1, 1] (reference, by normalization)
   - S462: [15.029, 9.290, 14.048]
   - S462TY: [7.068, 4.068, 4.496]
   - T265: [0.779, 0.163, 2.341]
   - STS-26T: [0.306, 0.221, 0.207]

D. COMPUTATION
   - For each MPNST line: mean, SD, and Welch's two-sample t-test vs HSC (equivalent here to a one-sample t-test against mu=1, since HSC has zero variance)
   - scipy.stats.ttest_ind(vals, hsc, equal_var=False)

E. RESULT
   - S462: mean=12.79, SD=3.07, t=6.65, p=0.0219 -> SIGNIFICANTLY ELEVATED
   - S462TY: mean=5.21, SD=1.62, t=4.49, p=0.0461 -> SIGNIFICANTLY ELEVATED
   - T265: mean=1.09, SD=1.12, t=0.15, p=0.8979 -> NOT SIGNIFICANT (high variance: 0.78, 0.16, 2.34; one replicate high but two below 1)
   - STS-26T: mean=0.24, SD=0.05, t=-24.43, p=0.0017 -> SIGNIFICANTLY REDUCED

   Paper claim: S462, S462TY, T265 have elevated SHH pathway activation.
   Data supports: S462 (yes), S462TY (yes), T265 (NOT statistically significant at p<0.05 with these 3 replicates, although mean 1.09 > 1). STS-26T is significantly reduced (used as negative control in paper).

F. CAVEATS
   - Very small n (3 replicates per line) limits statistical power, especially for T265 where replicates varied widely (0.16 to 2.34)
   - The HSC reference has zero variance by design (normalization), which inflates precision of Welch's test; the paper may have used a different statistical approach (e.g., log-transformed values, or one-sample against the arithmetic mean of log-transformed HSC replicates)
   - No multiple-testing correction applied; with Bonferroni across 4 tests (alpha=0.0125), S462TY would fall to borderline (p=0.046 > 0.0125) - though S462 and STS-26T still robust
   - The paper's claim that T265 has 'elevated SHH pathway activation' may rest on trends, multiple SHH-pathway genes together (GLI2, SMO, PTCH1), or additional experiments (e.g., chromatin/methylation data in Fig 5), not solely Fig 5j GLI1 qPCR
   - Sheet has only one gene's data (consistent with the user note specifying GLI1); GLI2/SMO qPCR not present in this sheet
